locked_facts: Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant (along a streamline)
Where speed is high, pressure is low (and vice versa)
Equation of continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (mass conservation for incompressible flow)
Narrower pipe → faster flow → lower pressure
Venturi effect: pressure drops in a constriction
Applies to ideal (inviscid, incompressible, steady, irrotational) flow
Bernoulli's equation is energy conservation per unit volume for flowing fluid
truth_statements: Bernoulli's principle states that for steady flow of an ideal fluid the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit volume remains constant
In a horizontal pipe where the cross-section narrows, the fluid speeds up and the pressure drops
The equation of continuity requires that fluid speed increases where the pipe cross-section decreases
Bernoulli's principle explains the lift on airplane wings: faster flow over the top creates lower pressure
The Venturi meter uses the pressure drop in a constriction to measure flow rate

aha_moment.what_to_show: A horizontal pipe with a constriction. By continuity: fluid speeds up in the narrow section. By Bernoulli: faster speed → lower pressure. Manometer tubes at wide and narrow sections show different heights. The 'high speed = low pressure' connection made visible.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Side-by-side manometers — wide section: high pressure (tall column), narrow section: low pressure (short column), with velocity arrows
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students expect high-speed fluid to exert MORE pressure — seeing the manometer drop at the constriction overturns this intuition

simulation_states.label: Continuity equation
simulation_states.description: Water flows through a pipe that narrows. Fluid speeds up in the narrow section. A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ shown. Particle tracers move faster in the constriction.
simulation_states.emphasis: What goes in must come out — narrower pipe means faster flow

simulation_states.label: Bernoulli's equation
simulation_states.description: Pressure gauges added. Wide section: high P, low v. Narrow section: low P, high v. P + ½ρv² = constant (horizontal). Energy bars shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Faster flow = lower pressure — energy is redistributed, not created

simulation_states.label: Venturi effect
simulation_states.description: Manometer tubes show pressure at different sections. In the constriction, the liquid column is shorter (lower pressure). Atomiser and Venturi meter principles demonstrated.
simulation_states.emphasis: The pressure drop in a constriction is the Venturi effect — used in carburettors and flow meters

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts pipe diameters and flow rate. Pressure and velocity at each section update. Height variation shows gravitational term ρgh. Applications: airplane wing, ball curve.
simulation_states.emphasis: Make the constriction narrower — pressure drops more. Add height difference — see ρgh term.

common_misconceptions.belief: Faster moving fluid exerts more pressure
common_misconceptions.correction: It's the opposite: faster moving fluid has LOWER pressure (for horizontal flow). P + ½ρv² = constant means if v increases, P must decrease. This is the core of Bernoulli's principle.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show pressure gauges dropping as velocity increases in a constriction

common_misconceptions.belief: Bernoulli's equation works for all fluids in all conditions
common_misconceptions.correction: Bernoulli's equation applies only to ideal flow: steady, incompressible, inviscid (no viscosity), and along a streamline. For viscous fluids, turbulent flow, or compressible gases at high speed, modifications are needed.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show viscous flow where Bernoulli prediction fails — pressure doesn't recover after constriction
